flashcards.Back: Cisco's default spanning tree mode that uses 802.1w (RSTP) logic while maintaining a separate spanning tree instance for every configured VLAN.
The IEEE standard for Rapid Spanning Tree Protocol, which provides significantly faster convergence than the original 802.1D STP by using a proposal-and-agreement process.
The central switch in a spanning tree topology. All other switches calculate their best path to this device, which is elected based on the lowest Bridge ID.
An 8-byte value used by STP to elect the root bridge. it consists of a 2-byte Priority (including VLAN ID) and the switch's 6-byte MAC address.
The single port on a non-root switch that has the lowest cumulative path cost to reach the root bridge.
The port on a network segment that is responsible for forwarding traffic from that segment toward the root bridge. Every active link has exactly one DP.
An RSTP port role for an interface that has an alternative path to the root bridge but is currently blocked. it can become a Root Port almost instantly if the primary fails.
An RSTP port role for an interface that provides a redundant path to a segment where the switch already has a Designated Port.
The RSTP port state that combines the functions of the legacy STP Blocking and Disabled states. it does not forward data frames or learn MAC addresses.
An intermediate RSTP state where the port does not yet forward data frames but begins to populate the MAC address table based on incoming traffic.
The operational RSTP state where the port is fully functional, forwarding data frames and learning MAC addresses.
A Cisco feature that allows a port connected to an end-user device to transition immediately to the forwarding state, bypassing the usual STP delay.
A security feature that automatically disables a PortFast-enabled port if it receives a BPDU, preventing loops from unauthorized switches.
A configurable value (default 32768) used to influence the root bridge election. it must be set in increments of 4096.
A 12-bit field within the Bridge Priority that carries the VLAN ID, allowing for a unique Bridge ID for every VLAN instance in PVST+.
The total cumulative cost of all links from a switch port to the root bridge. STP uses this value to determine the Root Port.
The specific management frame used by spanning tree to communicate topology information and maintain the root bridge election.
The state where all switches in a network have agreed on their port roles and states, and there is a single loop-free path between all nodes.
The RSTP term for a port connected to a host device. When PortFast is enabled on a port, RSTP treats it as an edge port.
The mechanism used by RSTP to achieve rapid convergence by allowing adjacent switches to explicitly negotiate port states rather than relying on timers.
A specialized BPDU sent by a switch to inform the root bridge that a link has failed or a new path has become active.
A full-duplex link between two switches. RSTP can only perform rapid transition to forwarding on these types of links.
A half-duplex link (typically connected to a hub). RSTP must fall back to legacy 802.1D timers on these links, slowing down convergence.
A Cisco IOS macro that automatically sets a switch's priority to 24576 (or lower) to ensure it becomes the root bridge for a specific VLAN.
A Cisco IOS macro that sets a switch's priority to 28672, positioning it to take over as root bridge if the primary root fails.
A verification command that displays the STP status, root bridge info, and port roles/states for a specific VLAN instance.
The legacy STP timer (default 20s) indicating how long a switch waits after missing a BPDU before triggering a topology change.
The legacy STP timer (default 15s) that defines the length of the Listening and Learning states. RSTP eliminates the need for this in most cases.
The global configuration command used to enable the Rapid Per-VLAN Spanning Tree Plus mode on a Cisco switch.
The older Cisco-proprietary version of spanning tree that uses 802.1D logic but provides a separate instance per VLAN.
